1: vayehi devar-YHWH elai lemor (wayhî dĕbar-Yhwh ʾēlay lēmōr): Chapters 25-32 constitute Ezekiel's oracles against foreign nations (oracles against the nations), a standard section in major prophetic books (Isa 13-23; Jer 46-51; Amos 1-2). Ezekiel's foreign-nation oracles are structurally positioned between the fall-of-Jerusalem oracles (Ezekiel 1-24) and the restoration oracles (Ezekiel 33-48), forming a central bridge: the nations that gloated over Israel's fall will themselves fall, and Israel's restoration will occur in their wake. Chapter 25 covers the four closest neighbors: Ammon, Moab, Edom, and Philistia.
2: ben-adam sim panekha el-bney ammon vehinnave alayhem (ben-ʾādām śîm pānêkā ʾel-bĕnê ʿammôn wĕhinnābēʾ ʾălêhem): Sim panekha el (set your face toward) — the prophetic posture of confrontational address. The same formula introduces oracles against Israel (Ezekiel 6:2; Ezekiel 13:17) and foreign nations (Ezekiel 29:2; Ezekiel 35:2). The physical orientation of the prophet's face is the visible embodiment of divine attention turning toward judgment. Ammon (modern Jordan, east of the Jordan River) was a persistent enemy of Israel from the judges period through the post-exilic era.
3: veamarta livney ammon shimau dvar Adonai YHWH ko amar Adonai YHWH yaan amarekh heach el-miqdashi ki nihal veal-admat Yisrael ki nishama veal-beit Yehudah ki halchu bagolah (wĕʾāmartā lĭbnê ʿammôn šimʿû dĕbar-ʾădōnāy Yhwh kōh ʾāmar ʾădōnāy Yhwh yaʿan ʾomrekem heʾāḥ ʾel-miqdāšî kî niḥāl): Heach! — the exclamation of malicious glee at another's misfortune, cognate to the German Schadenfreude. Three objects of Ammon's gleeful contempt: (1) YHWH's sanctuary when profaned; (2) the land of Israel when desolated; (3) the house of Judah when exiled. The tripling intensifies the charge: their joy was total, covering every dimension of Israel's catastrophe. Rejoicing over Israel's disaster is treated as an attack on YHWH himself — to mock the sanctuary is to mock the one who dwells there.
4: lachen hineni notencha livney-qedem lemorah vehotsivu tiroteyhem bekhekh veshamu mishkenoteyhem bekhekh hemah yochelu piryekh veimah yishtu chalvekh (lākēn hinnênî nōtēnĕkā lĭbnê-qedem lĕmôrāšâ wĕyōšĕbû ṭîrôtêhem bāk wĕnātĕnû mišĕkĕnôtêhem bāk hēmmāh yōʾklû piryēk wĕhēmmāh yišĕtû ḥalbēk): Bney-qedem (sons/people of the East) — nomadic desert peoples (cf. Genesis 29:1; Judges 6:3; Job 1:3). Ammon's land will become the possession of the very people who lived at Ammon's margins. The judgment is fitting: Ammon rejoiced over others losing their land; now Ammon loses its own. Tiroteyhem... mishkenoteyhem (encampments... dwellings) — the nomads will settle permanently in what was Ammon's territory.
5: venatati et-Rabbah lingveh gemalim veet-beny ammon lemeratch tson vidaatem ki ani YHWH (wĕnātatî ʾet-rabbāh lĭnwēh gĕmallîm wĕʾet-bĕnê ʿammôn lĕmirbaṣ ṣōʾn ûyĕdaʿtem kî ʾănî Yhwh): Rabbah (רַבָּה, modern Amman, Jordan) was Ammon's capital city, one of the most important cities in the Transjordan region. Its reduction to a neveh gemalim (pasture for camels) is the maximally degrading fate for an urban capital: from royal city to grazing ground. The recognition formula (vidaatem ki ani YHWH) attached to the judgment of a foreign nation is significant: even pagan nations will come to know YHWH through his judgments on Israel's enemies.
6: ki ko amar Adonai YHWH yaan meqekh kaf verakha regel vetismach bekol-sheat naphsheykha el-admat Yisrael (kî kōh ʾāmar ʾădōnāy Yhwh yaʿan meqekem kap̄ wĕrāqoʿa regel wĕtišmĕḥû bĕkol-šeʾat naphšĕkā ʾel-ʾadmat yiśrāʾēl): Meqekh kaf (clapping hands) and raqoa regel (stamping feet) — these are the physical expressions of contemptuous schadenfreude at Israel's destruction. The language implies a kind of victory dance: Ammon celebrated Israel's exile. The charge reinforces Ezekiel 25:3: Ammon's sin is not merely passive indifference but active, gleeful celebration of a covenant-neighbor's disaster.
7: lachen hineni natiti et-yadi alaykha untatikha levaz lagoyim vekhiratiykha min-haammim vehavdatiykha min-haaratsot veishmidtiykha vidaata ki ani YHWH (lākēn hinnênî nāṭîtî ʾet-yādî ʿālêkā ûnĕtattîkā lĕbaz laggôyim wĕkārattîkā min-hāʿammîm wĕhāʾabadtîkā min-hāʾărāṣôt wĕhišmadtîkā wĕyādaʿtā kî ʾănî Yhwh): Natiti et-yadi alaykha (I stretched out my hand against you) — the formula of divine judgment-action (cf. Ezekiel 6:14; Ezekiel 14:9; Ezekiel 16:27). The five verbs of judgment (give as plunder, cut off, destroy from lands, annihilate) cascade in total devastation. Importantly the recognition formula (vidaata ki ani YHWH, “you will know that I am the LORD”) is second-person singular, addressed to Ammon: Ammon will come to know YHWH through judgment on itself.
8: ko amar Adonai YHWH yaan amor Moav useir hineh keveit Yisrael kekol hagoyim (kōh ʾāmar ʾădōnāy Yhwh yaʿan ʾemar mōʾāb wĕśēʿîr hinnēh kĕbêt yiśrāʾēl kĕkol-haggôyim): Hineh keveit Yisrael kekol-hagoyim (“the house of Judah is like all the other nations”) — this is the insult that triggers Moab's judgment: the theological claim that Israel/Judah has no special covenantal status, that YHWH's election of Israel is void or never existed. This is the deepest possible attack on the covenant relationship. To say Israel is “like all the nations” is to deny YHWH's choice, YHWH's faithfulness, and YHWH's capacity to maintain a covenant people. The insult is against YHWH as much as against Israel.
9: lachen hineni potach et-keter Moav meheaarim meaareiv miaqtsar tifarto Beit HaYeshimot Baal Meon vKiriyataim (lākēn hinnênî p̄ōtēaḥ ʾet-kĕtep̄ mōʾāb mēhʿārîm mēʿārāyw miqqĕṣēhû tĕp̄ʾartô bêt hayyĕšimōt bĕʿal mĕʿôn wĕqiryātāyim): Keter Moav (the flank/shoulder of Moab) — the strategic frontier region. Beth-jeshimoth (house of the desolations), Baal-meon (lord of habitation), and Kiriathaim (double city) were the glory-cities of Moab's northern border — their capture would be the beginning of the end for Moab's territorial integrity. The divine opening of Moab's flank is the tactical precision of YHWH as military strategist against Israel's enemies.
10: livney qedem al-bney ammon unetatiyah lemorah lema-an lo-tizaker bney ammon bagoyim (lĭbnê qedem ʿal-bĕnê ʿammôn ûnĕtattîhā lĕmôrāšâ lĕmaʿan lōʾ-tizzāḵēr bĕnê ʿammôn baggôyim): Both Ammon and Moab receive the same judgment of being given to the “sons of the East.” The double judgment creates structural symmetry: two nations that insulted Israel (Ammon gleefully, Moab theologically) receive the same fate. Lema-an lo-tizaker (so that Ammon may not be remembered among the nations) — obliteration of national memory is the ultimate judgment for a nation: not merely defeat but total erasure from the historical record.
11: uvemoav esa shefatim videu ki ani YHWH (ûbĕmōʾāb ʾeʿśeh šĕp̄āṭîm wĕyādĕʿû kî ʾănî Yhwh): Esa shefatim (I will execute judgments) — the plural shefatim emphasizes the comprehensive and multiple nature of YHWH's judgment on Moab. The recognition formula is again applied to a pagan nation: the purpose of judgment is always ultimately knowledge of YHWH. Even through the destruction of Israel's enemies, YHWH's identity and character are being disclosed.
12: ko amar Adonai YHWH yaan asot Edom benakom levet Yehudah vayishmu asham vayinqemu bahem (kōh ʾāmar ʾădōnāy Yhwh yaʿan ʿăśôt ʾĕdôm bĕnāqōm lĕbêt yĕhûdāh wayyaʾšĕmû ʾāšôm wayyinqĕmû bāhem): Edom benaqom (Edom in taking vengeance) — Edom's sin is specifically named as naqqam (vengeance), amplified by vayashmu asham (incurred great guilt). Edom was Israel's brotherly enemy — descended from Esau — and their betrayal of Judah during the Babylonian siege (Psalms 137:7; Obad 10-14; Lamentations 4:21-22) was particularly bitter. Of all the nations judged in Ezekiel 25, Edom receives the most extensive treatment in Ezekiel (cf. Ezekiel 35) because the brotherly betrayal is theologically more significant than the foreign nations' hostility.
13: lachen ko amar Adonai YHWH venatatiy et-yadi el-Edom vehikhratiy mimenu adam uvehemah vanatatihu leshomamah miteyman uvdedanim becharev yipolu (lākēn kōh ʾāmar ʾădōnāy Yhwh ûnātatî ʾet-yādî ʾel-ʾĕdôm wĕhikrattî mimmennû ʾādām ûbĕhēmāh wĕnĕtattîhû lĕšāmemāh mîtêmān ûdĕdānāh baḥereb yippōlû): Teman (תֵּימָן, south/right-hand direction) was a region/city in northern Edom known for wisdom (Jeremiah 49:7; Obad 9). Dedan was in the south. The desolation “from Teman” to those of “Dedan” covers Edom's entire north-to-south extent — comprehensive territorial destruction. The combination of human and animal death (adam uvehemah) is the total desolation formula (cf. Jeremiah 7:20; Ezekiel 21:6), indicating the land itself becomes uninhabitable.
14: venatati et-niqmati beEdom beyad ami Yisrael veasu veEdom keapyi ukhemati veyadeu et-niqmati neum Adonai YHWH (wĕnātatî ʾet-niqmātî bĕʾĕdôm bĕyad ʿammî yiśrāʾēl wĕʿāśû bĕʾĕdôm kĕʾappî ûkăḥēmātî wĕyādĕʿû ʾet-niqmātî nĕʾum ʾădōnāy Yhwh): Niqmati (my vengeance) — YHWH personally executes vengeance on Edom. Uniquely here, the instrument is “my people Israel” — not the nations of the East or Babylonian armies. This anticipates the Obadiah and Malachi oracles about Edom's final fate. The recognition formula is formulated differently: not “they will know that I am the LORD” but “they will know my vengeance” — they will know YHWH through the experience of his retributive justice.
15: ko amar Adonai YHWH yaan asot Pelishtim beneqamah veyinqemu besha nefarsh vishammi leashmidam beyivat olam (kōh ʾāmar ʾădōnāy Yhwh yaʿan ʿăśôt pĕlištîm bĕnĕqāmāh ûnaqqĕmû bĕšāʾṭ nĕp̄ereš wĕyašḥîtû bĕʾēbat ʿôlām lĕmašĕḥît): Eyvah ʿôlam (אֵיבַת עוֹלָם, ancient/eternal enmity) — the Philistine hostility toward Israel is characterized as a permanent, unrelenting hatred rooted in antiquity. The Philistines are the paradigmatic enemy of Israel from the judges period through David's wars. Bisha nefresh (with malice/contempt of soul) — the Philistine revenge had a specifically psychological viciousness, not merely political opportunism. This ancient enmity is what the oracle holds them accountable for.
16: lachen ko amar Adonai YHWH hineni noteh yadi el-Pelishtim vehikhratiy et-Keretim vehiabadeti et-sherith hof hayam (lākēn kōh ʾāmar ʾădōnāy Yhwh hinnênî nōṭeh yādî ʿal-pĕlištîm wĕhikrattî ʾet-kĕrētîm wĕhāʾabadtî ʾet-šĕʾērît ḥôp̄ hayyām): Keretim (כְּרֵתִים, Cherethites) — a Philistine sub-group, often identified with Cretans (LXX reads “Cretans”; 1 Samuel 30:14; 2 Samuel 8:18). The Cherethites were among David's elite royal guard (2 Samuel 8:18; 1 Kings 1:38) — a remarkable use of Philistine mercenaries in Israel's service. Sherit hof hayam (remnant of the seacoast) — the Philistine coastal strip (Gaza, Ashkelon, Ashdod, Ekron, Gath) defined by its Mediterranean coastline. The seacoast that had been Philistia's geographic advantage is named as the boundary of its destruction.
17: veasiti vahem neqamot gedolot betochechet chemah veydeu ki ani YHWH benatati et-niqmati bahem (wĕʿāśîtî bāhem nĕqāmôt gĕdōlôt bĕtôkĕḥôt ḥēmāh wĕyādĕʿû kî ʾănî Yhwh bĕnitî ʾet-niqmātî bāhem): Neqamot gedolot betochechet chemah (great vengeance with wrathful rebukes) — the closing formula of the four-nation oracle section escalates to “great vengeance” against the Philistines, as though YHWH's judgment intensifies with each nation named. The final recognition formula — “they will know that I am the LORD when I lay my vengeance upon them” — frames the entire chapter 25 oracle unit: every judgment against every nation ultimately serves the disclosure of YHWH's identity as the sovereign LORD who keeps covenant-justice for his people.
